weaknesses.title: Improper Access Control
weaknesses.description: The product does not restrict or incorrectly restricts access to a resource from an unauthorized actor. This is the parent class for all access control weaknesses and represents the fundamental failure to enforce authorization policies. Improper access control can result in unauthorized data disclosure, modification, or destruction. It encompasses missing access controls, incorrectly implemented authorization logic, and access control mechanisms that can be circumvented. OWASP consistently ranks broken access control as the number one web application security risk.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Perform systematic access control testing by creating test accounts at each privilege level and verifying that each can only access authorized resources. Use automated tools to enumerate API endpoints and test each with different authorization levels. Check for horizontal privilege escalation by modifying resource identifiers in requests. Review access control implementation for consistent enforcement across all layers (presentation, business logic, data access). Test whether access control decisions are cacheable or bypassable through URL manipulation.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement centralized access control enforcement at the framework level using a policy decision point (PDP) and policy enforcement point (PEP) architecture. Deny access by default and require explicit grants. Use attribute-based access control (ABAC) or role-based access control (RBAC) consistently. Enforce access control checks on every request, not just at the UI level. Implement server-side authorization validation that does not rely on client-provided role or permission data. Log all access control failures for security monitoring. Conduct regular access control reviews and automated testing.

weaknesses.title: Improper Authorization
weaknesses.description: The product does not perform or incorrectly performs an authorization check when an actor attempts to access a resource or perform an action. Unlike CWE-284 which is the broader class, CWE-285 specifically addresses the failure of the authorization decision itself rather than the entire access control mechanism. This includes logic errors in permission evaluation, incorrect role-to-permission mappings, authorization decisions based on incomplete information, and failure to consider the full context of the access request including the resource owner, requestor permissions, and action type.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review authorization decision logic for completeness and correctness. Test with various user roles to verify proper permission enforcement. Check for authorization checks that only verify authentication but not specific permissions. Test business logic workflows for authorization bypass through parameter manipulation. Verify that authorization is enforced at the data layer, not just the presentation layer. Check for inconsistencies between documented access control policies and actual implementation.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement a well-defined authorization model (RBAC, ABAC, or ReBAC) with clear role-to-permission mappings documented and enforced in code. Use a centralized authorization service or middleware that evaluates authorization policies consistently. Ensure authorization checks consider the complete context: who is requesting, what resource is being accessed, and what action is being performed. Implement authorization unit tests that verify both positive (allowed) and negative (denied) cases. Use policy-as-code frameworks (Open Policy Agent, Cedar, Casbin) for complex authorization logic.

weaknesses.title: Improper Privilege Management
weaknesses.description: The product does not properly manage the assignment, tracking, or revocation of privileges for actors, leading to an accumulation of unnecessary privileges. Improper privilege management results in users, services, or processes having more access than required for their function, violating the principle of least privilege. This includes failure to revoke privileges when roles change, improper inheritance of privileges from parent roles, uncontrolled privilege escalation paths, and the absence of periodic access reviews. This is the root cause of many insider threat scenarios.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Conduct privilege audits to identify users with excessive permissions relative to their job functions. Compare assigned privileges against documented role definitions. Check for dormant accounts with active privileges. Review privilege escalation workflows for proper approval and time-limiting. Monitor for privilege creep by tracking privilege changes over time. Analyze group memberships in Active Directory/LDAP for nested groups that grant unintended permissions. Review OAuth2 scope assignments for overly broad access grants.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement a formal privilege management lifecycle: request, approve, provision, review, deprovision. Use role-based access control with well-defined roles that align with job functions and follow least privilege. Implement automated access reviews on quarterly cycles with attestation from resource owners. Use just-in-time (JIT) privilege escalation for administrative access with automatic expiration. Integrate with HR systems (via SCIM) to automatically adjust privileges when roles change or employees depart. Implement separation of duties for critical functions.

weaknesses.title: Privilege Context Switching Error
weaknesses.description: The product does not properly manage privilege levels when transitioning between different security contexts, potentially retaining elevated privileges when moving to a lower-privilege context or failing to acquire necessary privileges when moving to a higher-privilege context. This weakness is common in applications that implement impersonation, delegation, or run-as functionality. When the application switches between user contexts (e.g., processing a request on behalf of different users), it may inadvertently carry forward privileges from the previous context.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review code paths where the application switches between security contexts (impersonation, delegation, run-as operations). Verify that privilege state is completely reset when switching contexts. Test impersonation flows to ensure the impersonating process cannot access resources beyond the impersonated user's permissions. Check for leaked environment variables or thread-local storage that carries privilege state across context switches. Monitor for unexpected privilege usage after context transitions in audit logs.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement strict privilege isolation between security contexts using the operating system or runtime's native isolation mechanisms. When switching contexts, explicitly drop all privileges before acquiring the new context's privileges rather than inheriting from the previous context. Use separate processes or threads with distinct security contexts for operations requiring different privilege levels. Implement privilege boundary testing in automated tests. For Kerberos delegation, use constrained delegation (S4U2Proxy) rather than unconstrained delegation.

weaknesses.title: Privilege Dropping / Lowering Errors
weaknesses.description: The product attempts to drop privileges but does so incorrectly, effectively failing to lower the privilege level as intended. This occurs when applications that start with elevated privileges (e.g., root/SYSTEM for binding to low-numbered ports) fail to properly drop to a lower-privilege user after initialization. Common failures include dropping the effective UID but not the saved UID, failing to drop supplementary group memberships, not validating that privilege reduction succeeded, and error handling that restores elevated privileges.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review privilege-dropping code for completeness: check that both real and effective UIDs/GIDs are modified, supplementary groups are cleared, and the operation's success is verified. On Linux, check for proper use of setuid/setgid/setgroups in the correct order (groups first, then GID, then UID). Verify that the process cannot re-escalate after dropping privileges. Run the application and inspect the process's actual privilege level (/proc/PID/status) after the privilege drop should have occurred. Check for capabilities that survive the privilege transition.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement privilege dropping in the correct order: clear supplementary groups (setgroups), set GID (setgid/setresgid), then set UID (setuid/setresuid). Always verify the operation succeeded by checking return values and independently verifying the resulting privilege level. On Linux, drop unnecessary capabilities using prctl(PR_SET_NO_NEW_PRIVS) and seccomp. Use chroot or mount namespaces for filesystem isolation. Consider using systemd service hardening directives (PrivateTmp, NoNewPrivileges, ProtectSystem) for service deployment. Test privilege dropping in integration tests by verifying the process cannot access privileged resources after initialization.

weaknesses.title: Least Privilege Violation
weaknesses.description: The product operates with more privileges than necessary, violating the principle of least privilege. Running with excessive privileges means that if the application is compromised, the attacker inherits all those unnecessary privileges. This weakness applies to application processes running as root/Administrator unnecessarily, service accounts with broad permissions, database connections using DBA credentials, and API keys with full access scopes when read-only access would suffice. It is one of the most pervasive security weaknesses in enterprise environments.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Audit running processes to identify services operating as root/Administrator or with unnecessary elevated privileges. Review service account permissions in Active Directory, cloud IAM (Azure RBAC, AWS IAM, GCP IAM) for overly broad role assignments. Check database connection configurations for use of admin/dba credentials. Review OAuth2 token scopes for overly broad access grants. Analyze container security contexts for privileged mode or unnecessary capabilities. Use cloud security posture management (CSPM) tools to identify overprivileged identities.
weaknesses.remediation: Apply the principle of least privilege systematically: run application processes as dedicated non-root service accounts, grant database connections only the specific permissions needed (SELECT, INSERT on specific tables), use narrowly scoped OAuth2 access tokens, assign the minimum necessary cloud IAM roles. Use managed identities instead of service account keys where available. Implement just-in-time access for administrative operations. Run containers with non-root users and drop all capabilities except those explicitly needed. Conduct quarterly privilege reviews using automated tooling.

weaknesses.title: Improper Check for Dropped Privileges
weaknesses.description: The product attempts to drop privileges but does not verify that the operation succeeded before continuing execution with the assumption that privileges were dropped. System calls that drop privileges (setuid, setgid, setresuid) can fail silently in certain conditions, such as when resource limits prevent the operation or when security modules deny the transition. If the application does not check the return value and verify the resulting privilege level, it may continue to operate with full elevated privileges while believing it has reduced access.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review privilege-dropping code for missing return value checks on setuid/setgid/setresuid/setresgid calls. Check for error handling paths that silently continue execution on privilege drop failure. Verify that the application independently confirms the resulting privilege level (getuid/getgid/getgroups) after attempting to drop privileges. Test privilege dropping under resource-constrained conditions (RLIMIT_NPROC) to verify behavior when the operation fails. Analyze code paths after privilege drop calls for proper error handling.
weaknesses.remediation: Always check the return value of privilege-dropping system calls and abort execution if the operation fails. After calling setuid/setgid, independently verify the resulting privilege level using getuid/geteuid/getgid/getegid. Implement a fail-closed design where any failure in privilege dropping terminates the process rather than continuing with elevated access. Log privilege drop operations with before/after privilege levels. Use defensive programming patterns: after dropping to non-root, attempt to re-escalate and verify the attempt fails. Prefer setresuid/setresgid over setuid/setgid for more explicit control over real, effective, and saved IDs.

weaknesses.title: Improper Handling of Insufficient Privileges
weaknesses.description: The product does not properly handle situations where it lacks sufficient privileges to perform a requested operation, resulting in security-relevant errors or unintended behavior. When an application encounters an access denied error, it may fail open (grant access despite the error), expose sensitive information in error messages, enter an inconsistent state, or retry the operation with escalated privileges. Poor error handling around authorization failures is a common source of authorization bypass vulnerabilities.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Test how the application handles permission denied errors at various layers (filesystem, database, API, network). Inject authorization failures and verify the application responds securely without granting access. Check error handling code paths for logic that defaults to allowing access when authorization checks fail. Test whether verbose error messages reveal sensitive information about the authorization model or system internals. Verify that partial failures in multi-step operations do not leave the system in an inconsistent authorization state.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement fail-closed error handling where any authorization check failure results in access denial, never access grant. Return generic error messages to users that do not reveal details about the authorization model (e.g., return 403 without specifying which permission was missing). Log detailed authorization failure information server-side for debugging and security monitoring. Implement proper transaction rollback for multi-step operations that encounter authorization failures partway through. Design authorization error handling to maintain system invariants and prevent inconsistent states.

weaknesses.title: Incorrect Default Permissions
weaknesses.description: During installation or initial configuration, the product sets permissions for resources to values that grant more access than is appropriate, creating a window of vulnerability that persists until administrators manually tighten permissions. This is extremely common in newly deployed systems where default configurations prioritize ease of use over security. Examples include world-readable log files containing sensitive data, configuration files with overly broad read permissions, database instances with default administrative accounts, and cloud storage with public access enabled by default.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Audit filesystem permissions on application binaries, configuration files, log files, data directories, and TLS certificates/keys. Check cloud resource configurations for public access (S3 buckets, Azure Blob Storage, database firewall rules). Review default service account permissions in Kubernetes, Docker, and cloud platforms. Check umask settings in application startup scripts. Scan for world-readable sensitive files using automated security scanners. Review default IAM role assignments in cloud environments.
weaknesses.remediation: Set restrictive default permissions following the principle of least privilege: configuration files readable only by the application service account (0600), log directories writable only by the application (0700), TLS private keys readable only by the web server (0400). Use cloud-native permission management to enforce private-by-default for storage and services. Implement Infrastructure-as-Code with security-hardened baselines. Run automated compliance checks (CIS Benchmarks, cloud security scanners) to detect permission drift. Document expected permissions and verify them in deployment automation.

weaknesses.title: Improper Preservation of Permissions
weaknesses.description: The product does not properly preserve permissions when resources are copied, moved, backed up, or restored, resulting in unintended permission changes. When files or objects are moved between filesystems, extracted from archives, restored from backups, or transferred between systems, their original permission settings may be lost, replaced with default values, or inherited from the destination. This can result in sensitive resources becoming publicly accessible or restricted resources losing their access controls.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Test file operations (copy, move, archive/extract, backup/restore) to verify permissions are preserved. Check deployment automation for permission-setting steps after file transfer. Verify that cloud object storage ACLs are maintained during cross-region replication or cross-account copies. Review backup and disaster recovery procedures for permission preservation. Test container image build processes for proper permission settings on copied files. Audit CI/CD deployment pipelines for permission restoration steps.
weaknesses.remediation: Explicitly set permissions after any file or resource transfer operation rather than relying on permission preservation during copy/move. Include permission settings in deployment automation (Ansible, Terraform, Kubernetes manifests). Use Infrastructure-as-Code to define and enforce permissions declaratively. For archive operations, use formats that preserve permissions (tar) and verify after extraction. Implement automated compliance checks that verify resource permissions match expected values after any modification or deployment operation.

weaknesses.title: Incorrect Permission Assignment for Critical Resource
weaknesses.description: The product assigns incorrect permissions to a critical resource such as a configuration file, credential store, encryption key, or database, allowing unauthorized actors to read, modify, or delete the resource. This differs from CWE-276 (default permissions) in that it addresses explicitly assigned but incorrect permissions rather than default values. Common examples include TLS private keys with group or world read permissions, database files accessible to all users, application configuration files writable by non-privileged users, and API key files stored with overly permissive access controls.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Audit permissions on all critical resources: TLS certificates and private keys, database files, configuration files, credential stores, encryption key material, and backup files. Check for private keys with permissions more permissive than 0600. Verify that configuration files containing credentials are not readable by non-application users. Scan cloud storage permissions for sensitive data objects. Review Kubernetes secrets and ConfigMaps for overly broad RBAC access. Use automated security scanning tools to detect permission misconfigurations.
weaknesses.remediation: Assign restrictive permissions to all critical resources: private keys 0400 (read-only by owner), configuration files with credentials 0600 (owner read-write only), database files 0600. Use dedicated service accounts as resource owners rather than shared accounts. Implement automated permission verification in deployment pipelines. Use cloud-native secret management services with IAM-based access control rather than filesystem permissions. Implement runtime detection of permission changes on critical files using file integrity monitoring (AIDE, OSSEC, Wazuh).

weaknesses.title: Incorrect Authorization
weaknesses.description: The product performs an authorization check but the check is implemented incorrectly, allowing unauthorized access despite the presence of an authorization mechanism. This differs from CWE-862 (missing authorization) in that authorization logic exists but contains flaws. Common manifestations include checking the wrong user's permissions, using OR instead of AND for combined permission checks, checking authorization against cached/stale permissions, and race conditions between authorization checks and resource access. The presence of (broken) authorization logic can give false confidence.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Test authorization with carefully crafted test cases that cover boundary conditions: users at the edge of role hierarchies, users with partial permission sets, recently modified permissions, and concurrent access scenarios. Verify authorization checks use the authenticated user's identity from the server-side session, not from client-supplied parameters. Test for time-of-check/time-of-use (TOCTOU) race conditions in authorization. Review authorization code for logic errors such as inverted conditions, missing null checks, and improper boolean combinations.
weaknesses.remediation: Use well-tested authorization frameworks and libraries rather than custom authorization logic. Implement comprehensive authorization unit tests covering: valid access, invalid access, boundary conditions, role transitions, and concurrent access. Ensure authorization checks use the server-side authenticated identity, never client-supplied identity parameters. Implement authorization decisions as close to the resource as possible (at the data layer, not just the presentation layer). Use formal access control models (RBAC, ABAC) with verified implementations. Enable authorization audit logging to detect authorization logic failures in production.

weaknesses.title: Missing Authorization
weaknesses.description: The product does not perform an authorization check when an actor attempts to access a resource or perform an action. While CWE-306 addresses missing authentication, CWE-862 specifically addresses situations where the user is authenticated but no authorization check verifies whether they are permitted to access the specific resource or perform the specific action. This is extremely common in APIs where authentication is implemented but fine-grained authorization is missing, allowing any authenticated user to access any resource.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Systematically test every API endpoint with authenticated users of different privilege levels to verify authorization enforcement. Create a matrix of users, resources, and expected access outcomes and verify each combination. Focus on CRUD operations where users may be able to access, modify, or delete resources belonging to other users. Test administrative functions with non-administrative users. Use automated authorization testing tools that enumerate endpoints and test with different authentication contexts. Review middleware and filter configurations for missing authorization checks.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement authorization checks on every resource access and action, not just authentication verification. Use a framework-level authorization middleware that enforces checks automatically rather than relying on developers to add checks per-endpoint. Implement RBAC or ABAC policies that explicitly map which roles can access which resources and actions. Default to deny when no explicit authorization rule matches. Create automated authorization test suites that verify the complete access control matrix. Implement API gateway-level authorization as an additional enforcement layer.

weaknesses.title: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key (IDOR)
weaknesses.description: The system uses user-supplied input to directly reference internal objects (database records, file paths, user profiles) without verifying that the requesting user is authorized to access the referenced object. Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR) allow attackers to enumerate and access other users' data by manipulating identifiers in API requests, URLs, or form parameters. IDOR vulnerabilities are among the most commonly found and exploited access control weaknesses in modern web applications and APIs, often leading to mass data exposure.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Test all endpoints that accept resource identifiers (IDs, filenames, keys) by substituting identifiers of resources belonging to other users. Check for sequential integer IDs that can be easily enumerated. Test API endpoints by modifying path parameters, query parameters, and request body fields containing resource identifiers. Verify that authorization checks validate the requesting user's relationship to the referenced resource, not just authentication. Use automated scanning tools that detect IDOR by comparing responses between different user contexts for the same resource identifiers.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement object-level authorization checks that verify the requesting user's relationship to the referenced resource on every access. Use indirect reference maps that translate user-visible references to internal identifiers on the server side. Prefer UUIDs over sequential integers for resource identifiers to make enumeration impractical. Implement authorization middleware that automatically verifies resource ownership based on the authenticated user's identity. Add data-level filtering in database queries (WHERE tenant_id = :authenticated_tenant_id) as defense-in-depth. Log and alert on patterns of sequential ID enumeration attempts.

weaknesses.title: Improper Restriction of Names for Files and Other Resources
weaknesses.description: The product does not properly restrict the names or identifiers that users can assign to files, resources, or objects, allowing attackers to create resources with names that conflict with system resources, override security-relevant files, or create confusing naming collisions. In IAM contexts, this manifests as unrestricted naming of roles, groups, policies, or service accounts that can be used to impersonate system accounts or create confusion between user-created and system-managed identities.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Test resource creation with names that match system-reserved identifiers (admin, root, system, service-account-*). Check if users can create roles or groups with names identical to built-in or administrative roles. Verify that naming conventions distinguish between user-created and system-managed resources. Test with special characters, path separators, and encoding tricks in resource names. Review naming validation logic for bypass using Unicode normalization or case variations.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement strict naming conventions with reserved name lists that prevent user-created resources from using system-reserved names. Use namespace prefixes to distinguish user-created from system-managed resources (e.g., 'custom:' prefix for user-created roles). Validate resource names against an allowlist of permitted characters and patterns. Normalize names (case-folding, Unicode normalization) before uniqueness checks. Implement immutable resource identifiers separate from display names. Log resource creation with naming conflict detection alerts.

weaknesses.title: Execution with Unnecessary Privileges
weaknesses.description: The product performs an operation at a privilege level higher than the minimum required for the operation, expanding the potential impact of any exploitation. This commonly occurs when entire applications run as root/Administrator when only specific initialization operations require elevated privileges, when service accounts have broad administrative permissions for convenience, and when containerized applications run as root inside the container. The unnecessary privilege becomes the attacker's privilege if the application is compromised.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Audit running processes to identify those executing with elevated privileges (root, Administrator, high-privilege service accounts). Compare actual privileges against minimum required privileges for each process. Check container configurations for privileged mode, root user, and unnecessary Linux capabilities. Review cloud IAM role assignments for service accounts and workload identities. Analyze database connection permissions against actual query patterns. Monitor for privilege usage patterns that exceed expected boundaries.
weaknesses.remediation: Redesign applications to separate privileged initialization from runtime operations: perform privileged setup during startup, then drop to a minimal-privilege service account. Run containers with non-root users (USER directive in Dockerfile) and drop all capabilities except those explicitly needed (CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE, etc.). Use systemd hardening directives (CapabilityBoundingSet, NoNewPrivileges, PrivateTmp). Grant cloud IAM roles with minimum necessary permissions using custom roles when predefined roles are too broad. Implement privilege bracketing: escalate only for specific operations, then immediately drop.

weaknesses.title: Incorrect Privilege Assignment
weaknesses.description: The product assigns a privilege level to a user, process, or role that is higher than intended by the security policy, granting capabilities that the subject should not have. This differs from CWE-269 (Improper Privilege Management) in that the assignment itself is the error rather than the management process. Examples include accidentally adding users to administrative groups, assigning overly broad OAuth2 scopes, granting wildcard IAM permissions (Action: *), and misconfiguring role hierarchies that inherit more permissions than intended.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Compare assigned privileges against documented role definitions and job functions. Use cloud IAM analyzers (AWS IAM Access Analyzer, Azure Privileged Identity Management, GCP IAM Recommender) to identify overprivileged identities. Check for wildcard permissions in IAM policies. Review Active Directory group memberships, especially nested groups that may grant unintended transitive permissions. Audit OAuth2 scope assignments for overly broad access. Monitor privilege assignment events for assignments that exceed the requester's authority.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement formal privilege assignment workflows with approval chains and documentation. Use predefined role templates aligned with job functions rather than ad-hoc permission assignment. Enable cloud IAM recommendation features to right-size permissions based on actual usage. Implement automated drift detection that alerts when assigned privileges deviate from approved baselines. Use infrastructure-as-code for IAM configurations with code review requirements for privilege changes. Conduct quarterly access certification reviews where resource owners attest to the appropriateness of privilege assignments.

weaknesses.title: Privilege Defined With Unsafe Actions
weaknesses.description: A privilege is defined that includes the ability to perform inherently dangerous actions that should not be grouped with other permissions in the same privilege level. For example, a 'content editor' role that also includes the ability to manage user accounts, or a 'viewer' role that includes data export capabilities. When dangerous actions are bundled into broadly assigned roles, the blast radius of any account compromise increases dramatically because the compromised account inherits all bundled capabilities.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review role definitions for dangerous action bundles: identify roles that combine read access with write/delete capabilities, data access with administrative functions, or standard operations with security-sensitive capabilities. Check for cloud IAM policies that grant broad action sets (ec2:*, s3:*) when only specific actions are needed. Analyze role inheritance hierarchies for unintended capability accumulation. Review custom OAuth2 scopes for overly broad capability groupings.
weaknesses.remediation: Decompose roles into fine-grained permissions that separate dangerous actions from routine operations. Implement role separation following the principle of least privilege: create distinct roles for reading, writing, administering, and auditing. Require separate authentication or step-up authentication for dangerous actions even when the user has the necessary role. Use attribute-based access control (ABAC) to add contextual constraints to dangerous actions (e.g., allowing deletion only during maintenance windows). Document the security implications of each role's permissions.

weaknesses.title: Privilege Chaining
weaknesses.description: The product allows an attacker to chain together multiple legitimate privileges or actions that individually are authorized but when combined allow unauthorized access or actions. An attacker exploits a sequence of authorized operations to gradually escalate privileges or access resources that no single privilege would allow. For example, using file upload permission combined with script execution permission to achieve remote code execution, or using user creation permission combined with role assignment permission to create an administrative account.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Model the application's privilege structure as a graph and analyze for paths that allow privilege escalation through chains of individually authorized actions. Test common chaining patterns: create + assign roles, upload + execute, read credentials + authenticate, modify configurations + escalate. Monitor audit logs for sequences of actions that indicate privilege chaining attempts. Review API compositions that may allow chaining of individually safe operations into dangerous sequences. Analyze OAuth2 scope combinations that together provide broader access than intended.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement separation of duties that prevents the same identity from holding all privileges needed for a dangerous chain. Require additional authorization (step-up authentication, approval workflow) when a sequence of actions matches a known privilege chaining pattern. Implement rate limiting and anomaly detection on privilege-sensitive operations. Design authorization models that consider the cumulative effect of permission combinations, not just individual permissions. Use blast radius analysis during role design to identify and mitigate potential chaining vectors.

weaknesses.title: Unverified Ownership
weaknesses.description: The product does not properly verify that the user performing an operation on a resource is the owner of that resource or has explicit permission from the owner. This weakness goes beyond simple authorization checks by specifically addressing ownership verification, which is critical in multi-tenant environments and shared platforms. Without ownership verification, users may be able to modify, delete, or transfer resources belonging to other users or tenants, even when general authorization checks are in place.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Test all resource operations (read, update, delete, transfer) by attempting to perform them on resources owned by other users. Verify that the application checks the authenticated user's relationship to the resource, not just their role. Test multi-tenant isolation by attempting to access resources in other tenants. Check for IDOR vulnerabilities that bypass ownership checks. Verify that API endpoints filter resources by authenticated owner/tenant rather than returning all resources. Test delegation and sharing features for proper authorization propagation.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement ownership verification as a mandatory authorization check on all resource operations. In multi-tenant systems, enforce tenant isolation at the data layer using row-level security, tenant-scoped database connections, or mandatory WHERE clauses. Use the authenticated identity from the server-side session for ownership verification, never client-supplied owner IDs. Implement sharing and delegation mechanisms that explicitly grant access while maintaining ownership tracking. Log all ownership verification failures and cross-tenant access attempts for security monitoring.

weaknesses.title: Incorrect User Management
weaknesses.description: The product does not properly manage user accounts throughout their lifecycle, including creation, modification, disabling, and deletion, leading to security gaps such as orphaned accounts, accounts with stale permissions, or improperly provisioned accounts. This weakness encompasses failures in the user identity lifecycle: onboarding without proper verification, missing deprovisioning when employees depart, accounts remaining active after role changes, and inconsistent user states across federated systems.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Audit user accounts for: accounts without recent login activity that may be orphaned, accounts not associated with active employees (compare against HR system), accounts with inconsistent states across systems (disabled in AD but active in applications), and accounts created outside the normal provisioning workflow. Review user deprovisioning procedures and test that account disable/delete propagates across all connected systems. Check for service accounts without documented owners.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement automated user lifecycle management integrated with HR systems via SCIM or similar protocols. Automate account deprovisioning when employees depart, with immediate disabling and 30-day deletion. Implement periodic access reviews (quarterly for standard users, monthly for privileged users) with attestation from managers. Use Just-in-Time provisioning through federation protocols to reduce account sprawl. Maintain a service account registry with documented owners and regular attestation. Implement anomaly detection for unused accounts and alert after 90 days of inactivity.

weaknesses.title: Insufficient Granularity of Access Control
weaknesses.description: The product implements access control but with insufficient granularity, applying uniform access controls to resources that require different levels of protection. This results in either overly permissive access to sensitive resources (to accommodate less sensitive resources in the same category) or overly restrictive access to non-sensitive resources (to protect sensitive resources in the same category). In API contexts, this manifests as coarse-grained scopes that grant access to entire API surfaces when only specific endpoints are needed.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review access control policies for granularity: check if permissions are defined at the resource level or only at the category/module level. Analyze OAuth2 scopes for overly broad grants (e.g., 'admin' scope granting access to all administrative functions). Review RBAC role definitions for roles that combine many unrelated permissions. Test whether users granted access to one resource type automatically gain access to related but more sensitive resource types. Check cloud IAM policies for resource-level vs service-level permissions.
weaknesses.remediation: Design access control policies at the finest practical granularity: per-resource, per-action, per-field where appropriate. Implement fine-grained OAuth2 scopes (e.g., 'users:read', 'users:write', 'users:admin' instead of just 'users'). Use field-level access control for sensitive data fields within resources. Implement ABAC when RBAC granularity is insufficient, using attributes like resource sensitivity, time of access, and access context. Design API authorization to support resource-level permissions from the start, as retrofitting granular access control is significantly more difficult.

weaknesses.title: Internal Asset Exposed to Unsafe Debug Access Level
weaknesses.description: The product exposes internal assets or debug interfaces with access levels that are inappropriate for the deployment environment. Debug and diagnostic interfaces often bypass normal access controls, expose sensitive internal state, and provide capabilities far beyond what is needed for troubleshooting. When these interfaces are accessible in production environments without proper authentication and authorization, they create a critical attack surface. Common examples include exposed debug endpoints, profiling interfaces, and administrative consoles left in debug mode.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Scan production deployments for exposed debug endpoints (/debug, /trace, /profiler, /actuator, /graphql-playground, /_debug). Check for debug mode flags in production configurations. Verify that diagnostic interfaces are not accessible from external networks. Test whether debug endpoints bypass authentication and authorization controls. Check for verbose error messages, stack traces, and debug headers in production responses. Review framework configurations for debug mode settings (DEBUG=true, RAILS_ENV=development, NODE_ENV=development).
weaknesses.remediation: Implement environment-aware configuration that automatically disables debug interfaces in production. Use feature flags or environment variables to control debug functionality with fail-safe defaults (debug off). Restrict debug endpoints to internal networks using network-level controls and additionally protect with strong authentication. Remove debug code, endpoints, and dependencies from production builds entirely. Implement automated deployment checks that verify debug features are disabled before allowing production deployment. Use separate debug builds that are never deployed to production environments.

weaknesses.title: Insecure Inherited Permissions
weaknesses.description: The product uses an inheritance model for permissions where child objects inherit permissions from parent objects, but the inheritance mechanism grants more access than intended or does not allow sufficient control over what is inherited. Permission inheritance is common in filesystems, Active Directory, cloud IAM, and hierarchical authorization models. When inheritance is not properly controlled, granting permissions at a parent level can inadvertently expose sensitive child resources that should have more restrictive access.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Review permission inheritance hierarchies in Active Directory OUs, cloud resource hierarchies (Azure Management Groups, AWS Organizations OUs, GCP folders), and filesystem trees. Check for overly broad permissions set at parent levels that propagate to sensitive child resources. Identify resources where inheritance should be blocked but is not. Audit effective permissions on sensitive resources by tracing through the entire inheritance chain. Test whether breaking inheritance is properly implemented when resources require different permissions than their parents.
weaknesses.remediation: Design permission hierarchies with inheritance-aware planning: set the most restrictive common permissions at parent levels and grant additional permissions at child levels where needed. Implement inheritance blocking on resources that require permissions different from their parent. Regularly audit effective permissions by resolving the complete inheritance chain. Use explicit permission assignments rather than relying on inheritance for sensitive resources. Document the inheritance model and its security implications. Implement automated monitoring for inheritance changes that could inadvertently expose sensitive resources.

weaknesses.title: Improper Handling of Insufficient Permissions or Privileges
weaknesses.description: The product does not properly handle or respond to conditions where a user or process does not have sufficient permissions to complete an operation, potentially leading to unexpected behavior, information leakage, or security bypass. When insufficient permission conditions are not handled correctly, applications may expose detailed error information about the permission model, enter inconsistent states, or silently skip authorization checks. This is distinct from CWE-274 in that it focuses on the handling logic rather than the initial privilege state.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Test application behavior when authorization is denied: check for information leakage in error responses (detailed permission requirements, role names, internal identifiers). Verify that permission errors result in consistent HTTP status codes (403 for authorization failures). Test whether permission denied conditions are handled at all layers or only at the presentation layer. Check for silent failures where operations proceed without proper authorization when errors occur. Review error logging for proper attribution of authorization failures.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement consistent and secure handling of authorization failures across all application layers. Return generic 403 responses without details about the specific permission that was denied. Log detailed authorization failure information server-side with the authenticated identity, requested resource, required permission, and actual permissions. Ensure authorization failures always result in the operation being denied (fail-closed). Implement proper error handling that maintains application state consistency after authorization failures. Use centralized error handling for authorization to ensure consistent behavior.

weaknesses.title: Direct Request (Forced Browsing)
weaknesses.description: The web application does not adequately enforce appropriate authorization on all restricted URLs, scripts, or files, allowing users to access resources by directly requesting them rather than navigating through the intended workflow. Forced browsing attacks bypass access controls that are only enforced through the removal of navigation elements (links, buttons) rather than server-side authorization checks. Attackers enumerate URLs using wordlists, predictable naming patterns, or information from robots.txt, sitemap.xml, and JavaScript source files.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Use directory brute-forcing tools (dirbuster, gobuster, feroxbuster) against the application to discover resources not linked in the navigation. Test whether hidden or administrative pages are accessible by directly requesting their URLs. Review JavaScript files and source maps for references to undocumented endpoints. Check robots.txt and sitemap.xml for URLs that should be restricted. Verify that removing UI elements does not represent the sole access control mechanism. Test every discovered URL with different authentication and authorization contexts.
weaknesses.remediation: Implement server-side authorization checks on every resource request, not just those accessible through the UI navigation. Use a deny-by-default authorization model where all resources require explicit authorization grants. Remove all sensitive resources from web-accessible directories unless they are explicitly intended to be served. Implement URL-based access control at the web server or reverse proxy level as defense-in-depth. Ensure robots.txt does not reveal the existence of sensitive paths. Configure custom 403/404 responses that do not reveal whether a resource exists.

weaknesses.title: Improperly Controlled Modification of Dynamically-Determined Object Attributes
weaknesses.description: The product receives input that defines or modifies object attributes but does not properly control which attributes can be modified, allowing attackers to set security-relevant attributes they should not have access to. In IAM contexts, mass assignment vulnerabilities allow attackers to modify role assignments, permission flags, account status (active/disabled), tenant associations, or privilege levels by including unexpected fields in API requests. This is commonly known as mass assignment or auto-binding vulnerability.
weaknesses.detection_guidance: Test API endpoints that accept user input for modifying objects by including additional fields beyond those intended: role, admin, is_admin, permissions, tenant_id, active, verified, password, and other security-relevant fields. Review data binding configurations for allowlist vs denylist approaches. Check for automatic model binding that maps all request parameters to object properties. Test SCIM endpoints for ability to modify attributes outside the user's authorization scope. Review API schemas for undocumented writable fields.
weaknesses.remediation: Use explicit allowlists (whitelists) of modifiable attributes for each API endpoint and user role. Never use automatic model binding that maps all request fields to object attributes. Implement separate DTOs (Data Transfer Objects) for each operation that include only the fields that should be modifiable. For SCIM provisioning, validate that the requesting application is authorized to modify each attribute being set. Implement server-side validation that rejects requests containing unauthorized attributes. Log attempts to modify protected attributes as potential attack indicators.
